claim: The 0.8C steel exhibited a highest tensile strength of 979 MPa among the three steels.
evidence_text: the 0.8C steel exhibited a highest tensile strength of 979 MPa

claim: Fe–(18–28)Mn–(9–12)Al–(0.7–1.2)C triplex steels were reported to exhibit a strength of 700–1100 MPa and total elongation up to 60% or more.
evidence_text: Frommeyer et al. [1] investigated the high-strength Fe–(18–28)Mn–(9–12)Al–(0.7–1.2) C triplex steels exhibiting a strength of 700–1100 MPa and a total elongation up to 60% or more.

claim: The tensile ductility of Fe–20Mn–10Al–xC alloys first increased, reached a maximum of 59.1% at 1.0% C, and then declined to 17.1% at 1.5% C.
evidence_text: the tensile ductility of the steels first increased, reached a maximum value (59.1%) at 1.0% C and then declined again (only 17.1%) at 1.5% C.

claim: A duplex Fe–20Mn–9Al–0.6C steel exhibited a tensile strength of 800 MPa and an elongation of 46%.
evidence_text: Hwang et al. [8] studied a duplex Fe–20Mn–9Al–0.6C steel exhibiting a tensile strength of 800 MPa and an elongation of 46%.

claim: The Fe–27Mn–12Al–0.8C steel contained ordered B2 and DO phases in ferrite and κ carbide in austenite, and exhibited a yield strength of 812 MPa, tensile strength of 955 MPa, and elongation of 42%.
evidence_text: Another work [10] demonstrated that the Fe–27Mn–12Al–0.8C steel contained ordered phases in both phases, B2 and DO in ferrite and κ carbide in austenite, in which a relatively high yield strength of 812 MPa and tensile strength of 955 MPa were achieved, with a moderate elongation of 42%.

claim: The 0.5C steel has a measured composition of Fe–17.71Mn–9.90Al–0.48C (wt%).
The 0.8C steel has a measured composition of Fe–17.14Mn–9.91Al–0.77C (wt%).
The 1.2C steel has a measured composition of Fe–17.45Mn–10.19Al–1.16C (wt%).
The stacking fault energy of austenite is 73.4 mJ/m² for 0.5C, 77.9 mJ/m² for 0.8C, and 84.5 mJ/m² for 1.2C steel.
evidence_text: The stacking fault energy (SFE) values of austenite in 0.5C and 0.8C steels are 73.4 and 7 7 . 9 m J / m 2 and that of 1.2C steel is 8 4 . 5 m J / m 2 according to the model we used previously [13].

claim: Tensile tests were conducted at room temperature with a constant initial strain rate of 1.33×10⁻³ s⁻¹.
evidence_text: Tensile tests were carried out at room temperature using a universal testing machine (SANSCMT5000) operating at a constant crosshead displacement velocity (equivalent to an initial strain rate of 1 . 3 3 × 1 0 - 3 s - 1 ) .

claim: TEM foils were prepared by twinjet electropolishing using 9% perchloric acid in methanol at −30 °C, and examined at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.
evidence_text: Thin foils for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were prepared by twinjet electropolishing by using 9% perchloric acid in methanol at - 3 0 °C . TEM analysis was performed in a Tecnai G2 20 TEM at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV.

claim: In 0.8C steel, κ-carbide (Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ precipitates are present in austenite, and B2 domains with fine DO₃ particles are present in ferrite.
evidence_text: Further indexing of these SADPs confirms that 0.8C steel contains a κ carbide phase [ ( F e , M n ) 3 A l C x ] in austenite (Fig. 2 (c)) [14], and some B2 domains with fine DO3 particles in ferrite (Fig. 2(e))[10].

claim: At a true strain of 0.18 in 0.5C steel, Taylor lattices and microbands develop in austenite, while dense dislocation tangles appear in ferrite.
evidence_text: As deformation proceeds to a true strain of 0.18, intensive dislocation slip appears ( F i g . 6 ( c ) ) , and Taylor lattice ( F i g . 6 ( d ) ) and microbands (Fig. 6(e)) have developed in austenite; whereas dense dislocation tangles can be observed in ferrite (Fig. 6(f)).

claim: 0.5C steel exhibits total elongation of 32.8%, yield strength of 614.9 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 856.7 MPa, and UTS×TE product of 28100 MPa·%.
evidence_text: Table 2 Mechanical properties of 0.5C, 0.8C and 1.2C steels. 0.5C TE 32.8( ± 0.6) YS 614.9( ± 1.0) UTS 856.7( ± 0.7) UTS × TE 28100

claim: 0.8C steel exhibits total elongation of 38.4%, yield strength of 711.2 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 979.1 MPa, and UTS×TE product of 37597 MPa·%.
1.2C steel exhibits total elongation of 56.2%, yield strength of 735.1 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 946.7 MPa, and UTS×TE product of 53205 MPa·%.
The 0.8C steel achieves a specific strength of 142.3 MPa cm³/g.
evidence_text: Its specific strength is 142.3 MPa cm^3 / g , which is also quite competitive.

claim: At ε=0.18, 0.5C steel develops Taylor lattice and microbands in austenite, and dense dislocation tangles in ferrite (Fig. 6(c-f)).
evidence_text: In the second stage, a low energy Taylor lattice structure, ... and microbands develops in austenite (Fig. 6(d) and (e)), and more dislocation tangles form in ferrite (Fig. 6(f))

claim: In 1.2C steel, planar slip develops in the first stage, followed by formation of Taylor lattice and microbands in the second stage, and interaction between microbands in the final stage, corresponding to the three-stage hardening (Fig. 8).
evidence_text: the planar slip develops in the primary deformation stage (Fig. 8(a)), followed by the formation of Taylor lattice (Fig. 8(b)) and microbands (Fig.8(c)) (intermediate stage) and the interaction between microbands in the final stage (Fig. 8(d)).
